sentences.text: I was born in Duren, Germany.
D-U-E-R-E-N, which is in the Rhineland.
It was September 17, 1929.
It was a small town, an industrial town.
It was located between the major cities of Cologne and Aachen, very close.
Aachen is very close to the Belgian border.
And it was a quiet town.
And for culture and things of that nature, we could always go to Cologne and take part in museums and operas and concerts and things of that nature.
But essentially, it was a quiet, small town.
We had our own home-- my father and my brother, my mother, myself.
It was an attached home, which my father had built after the depression.
And we were very comfortable.
It was a nice part of town.
It was located immediately across the street from the Catholic hospital.
And so for as a young girl, I was able to go over there and hold new babies in the hospital and do a bit of snooping around about what it was all about.
And the nuns were always very, very kind and very generous and sort of encouraged me to do that.
In that particular house--
My parents, my brother, and myself.
My mother's name was Anna Marx, and my father's name is Rudolf Marx, and my brother was Eric Marx.
He was three years older than I was.
My father was in business with his older brother.

[SIGHS] They owned a factory, a textile factory, sorting factory, that employed about 100 people because Duren was very well known for their paper manufacturing and other products that are made from textiles.
And so it was an ideal location.
And I could go down to visit the factory and sit in the secretary's office.
[LAUGHS] And it was a very comfortable and a very lovely feeling for me.
I had four older cousins, considerably older, men, boys and my brother.
And so being the only youngest girl in the family, I got a bit of special treatment from everybody-- in other words, spoiled rotten [LAUGHS] and encouraged in my little pursuits at the time.
I attended a Jewish school which was connected to the synagogue.
And, as I understand, that was a school that had existed for many, many years.
And the first four grades of elementary school were one large classroom.
And the Cantor of the synagogue was in charge of both German teaching, and then we had our Hebrew classes and our lessons in religion.
Yes.
Yes, my father came from an orthodox family.
My grandmother [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and the rest of the siblings-- there were four brothers and one sister-- all lived in the town.
And, indeed, we were what is known as contributing and, perhaps, pillars of the Jewish community.
There were a hundred, about 120 Jewish families.
Indeed, we were involved.
I suppose it would be considered conservative Judaism as it is practiced here in the US.
Actually, all of them except for one uncle and his wife who were living in Amsterdam.
The rest of them were all around us.
It was a very close-knit family-- my grandmother, the matriarch, at the head of it.
And--
Her name was Auguste Marx.
And she-- she ruled us-- [LAUGHS] she ruled us all.
She was enormously patient with me but also stern.
And I-- the house where she lived was not far from the school and the synagogue.
And so after school I generally always went there and had my cookies and milk and a little extra spoiling and that sort of thing.
In the same building, my father's older, again, an older brother was a physician, general practitioner.
And he had his office in the same house.
His name was Karl Marx, and he was my very favorite uncle.
And also, upstairs lived the only sister in my father's family and her husband.
And she ran my grandmother's household since my grandmother was already in her 80s when I knew her.
So that there was my aunt and uncle and my grandmother and then my uncle the physician who lived in the same house.
Yes, my aunt was Clementine-- Clementine Bender and her husband Bertold Bender [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and then my grandmother and my uncle.
Not far away from there lived the uncle who was in business with my father together.
And this is someone who was very much involved in our lives both before and after the Holocaust.
His name was Moritz Marx.
His wife was Johanna.
And they had three sons-- Paul, Walter, and Karl Heinz.
Karl Heinz was the youngest and closer in age to me.
And he's the one that I grew up with as a [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] closer-- closer relative than the other two boys because they were already away at school and university.
In the family?
Oh, lots.
I mean, every weekend we would generally go out into the country, either to a stream or a picnic.
We always went on outings either together or with smaller groups or whatever.
And we went away on a vacation together with my aunt and uncle and the cousins and had really a comfortable and comfortable and, I would say, very happy kind of a existence.
I-- I couldn't think of anything that I would have done different in my youth.
It was a wonderful foundation, having the influence of all these loving relatives and that I was extremely attached to.
High holidays, we were always at my grandmother's house.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was-- it was always both a religious experience and a fun experience because we children always did a certain amount of cutting up.
Yeah, he, [SIGHS] not only that he shepherded me through-- through my tomboy years and patched me up [LAUGHS] whenever I got myself into trouble.
But he was also a-- a mentor.
And he had put one of my cousins through medical school, and it was sort of a foregone conclusion that he was also going to put me through medical school.
In the days of horse and buggies, I used to go on rounds with him to see patients in the countryside.
Later on, he had a car.
And it was always a [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] informative experience how he related to people and how-- how the people in the town really respected him a great deal.
And when I was at my grandmother's, I usually went downstairs and moseyed around his office.
And he told me about the sterilizer and all the different things that he uses when he sees patients and so forth.
So there was a very close bond.
[LAUGHS] Yes.
He-- he-- he had to check me for broken bones when I jumped out of unexpected places.
And of course, he was always at my side when I was sick.
I happen to have had diphtheria and was very, very sick.
And he was at my bedside day and night looking after me through those first crucial hours when you have the high fever and when you're very, very sick.
So it was very comforting to know that, ultimately, no matter what I did, [LAUGHS] he would always be there to take care of me.
And I did give him some hard times.
I-- When was the first time that you noticed that there was any wave of anti-Semitism?
My family undoubtedly noticed early on.
My friends and my activities-- I don't really-- I can't really say that before 1938 and Kristallnacht, that I was subjected to any overt expressions of anti-Semitism.
I know that my older cousins told me later on, many years later, that they had confrontations in the schoolyard as early as 1935.
But essentially, it was a quiet town, and I really was not aware of any overt expressions of anti-Semitism.

I don't ever even recall being called a dirty Jew, which is something that was quite common then. 1938, Kristallnacht was of course the turning point when neighbors turned away and didn't speak to us.
My father was arrested.
We had a cleaning lady for many, many years-- a lovely, lovely woman-- a good Christian who tried to prevent the-- the German, the SA, from-- as they were called-- arresting my father.
To no avail, he was taken away.
Do you remember the date of that?
That was November 9, 1938.
And he was taken away.
I was at home.
We were sitting at the dinner table.
And they came in, forced their way in, and just took him away without any-- just took him out.
There was no preparation of any kind, any explanation of any kind.
They just arrested him, period.
And then the phones began to ring.

And our relatives who lived closer to town-- we were a little bit on the outskirts-- called and said that under no circumstance should we be permitted, the children should be permitted, to go to school because the synagogue was burning and just to stay very close and in the house and protected.
I don't remember.
I assume they were calls from my uncle who would have called my mother.

And then also told her that she could not stay alone in the house in the outskirts, that he wanted her to come, to take the children, pack a few things, and come and live in the house with my grandmother and my relatives.
And there, which we did, and actually, physically, I lived in the house.
I shared my Aunt Clemmie's apartment.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were there for practically six months.
Very little.
Just barely because almost immediately the factory was confiscated.
Our house was confiscated.
My mother made trips to the house and packed essentials.
And we moved into town to be together with the family-- be more protected.
My father was not there.
And I do remember my mother crying a lot.

And also going out [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] on business, so to speak, that she had to get out, where in fact she was going to the officials and the bureaus and all different places trying to find out what had happened to my father and to get some sort of information.
He had been taken to Buchenwald, and she was allowed to bring a small bag of immediate personal effects.
And after that, there was no further-- no further contact at all.
At that and we-- we made the best of things.

There was a night when I do remember a brick flew through one of the windows, and we moved our beds closer to the inner wall and were more alert as to not being outdoors alone or not at all if possible, that sort of thing.
And-- which was very perplexing for a child because this was the neighborhood that I had moved through freely and talked to people in the shops and the stores.
And suddenly, it was all restricted.

It so happened that at the time, my father's brother, who was in the business with him, was in Bordeaux, France to do a business transaction along with my cousin who was going to be in the business as well.

And I do remember the frantic calls where, in code, my aunt tried to tell him that-- not to come home, you know, to have a good time, and go such and such a place, and visit a relative in Paris, in other words, trying to get the message out that he was not to return with my older cousin.
And--
Uncle Moritz, Maurice, in French.
And-- which in fact he understood, knowing that things were going on.
And he then went to stay with relatives in Paris.
So that he had to take care of getting my aunt and my younger cousin out of the country.
And he got them to Paris as well.
As for us, we had-- my parents had applied for a visa to the United States two years earlier.
My mother's youngest brother was in the United States since 1933, and he had sponsored us and put all that on track.
But since it was a fairly high number, it wasn't anywheres near being called, and therefore, we were in the situation.
There was a very wonderful Christian business associate of my father's--
And his name is Herbert Kramer, who lived in-- in Aachen, and a wonderful woman by the name of Christina Bianbaer, a young woman.
And this business associate, Herbert, made it his business to help my mother.
And he went with her to the official places to get information as to where my father was.
And then, on a somewhat [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] invented or manufactured excuse, he said he needed my father for his signature and the signing over of the factory to whoever it was going to go to.
And so he was released from Buchenwald on the basis of this-- this-- this legal transaction.
That release was actually early in November.
It came just in time for his birthday.
And--
And the year?
He was released in 1938, November of 1938.
And had he not appeared so totally broken and totally emotionally beaten, that it would have been a nice birthday present.
But in essence-- when I-- I only saw him very briefly on that occasion, and his head was shaved, and his teeth had been extracted, and he was scarred.
And he spoke very, very little.
In fact, at first I really didn't recognize him when I saw him.
And then after the signing, this business associate, who had dual citizenship in Belgium, literally smuggled him out of the country.
And being close to the Belgian border, it was fairly easy.
He was acquainted with the border guards, and he spirited him out of Germany at night and smuggled him into Belgium.
In Belgium he was arrested because he wasn't a Belgian natural, and he had no papers to be in Belgium.
But I remember years later my father saying it was a pleasure to be in a Belgian jail where everybody was very sympathetic.
He was in a small town called Spa, which was directly across the border.
And as it turned out, he had traveled in the business a lot to Holland and Belgium and also France, and he was fluent in French.
It so happened that one of his best customers and finest friends was also living in Spa.

And so he not only had personal help there, once he got there, but he also had financial help because knowing the business and so forth these people were very generous in lending money to support us when we went over.
And again, in March of 1939, the same Christian couple spirited us out of the country as well and brought my mother and my brother and myself illegally into Belgium.
Yes, it was interesting.
I remember we were prepared.
I had never laid eyes on either one of these two people.
I wasn't supposed to be able to recognize them, and I had a fairly big mouth [LAUGHS] and also remembered things.
And so the first leg was on the train.
And-- Christina--
My mother, my brother, and myself.

And in some distance, but in the same car, was Christina who was supposed to make sure that everything was going well according to plan, that if something went wrong, she would be able to report it to Herbert at the meeting point where he was going to pick us up with his car.
And she was wearing my mother's watch and her jewelry.
And at a very critical moment when the conductor was coming through the train, I spoke up in a very loud language and said, oh, look, Mommy.
This lady, she's got the same watch and the same necklace that you have.
They're identical.
And I think my mother just barely managed [LAUGHS] to shush me up in time and-- and end the subject.
We then met Herbert Kramer at a small station where we got off the train and got into his car.
And then--
No, not really.
We-- we had the-- my mother had a passport with a large J on the-- on the passport itself.
And other than that, we were really illegal in Belgium.

The way that she had procured these-- these permits was that the-- Christina went-- there was a little bit of a look-a-like, not terribly so but close enough-- that Christina used to take these things to the official bureaus, and they would stamp them and OK them and not put a J for Jewish on these papers and therefore OK them.
And then afterwards, somehow the J got onto the papers as we left.
But she did all this type of thing which enabled-- which enabled us really to do it.
If the J had been in there, we may never have, you know, been able to get out of the country.
So it was her work behind the scenes and, of course, Herbert's work behind the scenes that made it possible for us to get out.
We had no-- we-- we had no papers from the American consulate that would have said that we were en route to the United States.
I know that at one point before we left, there was a bit of hysteria, and my mother actually bought papers, or passages for all four of us, for a good deal of money, to go to Cuba.
If all else failed, we would have gone that route.
I wonder.
But at any rate, it was supposed to be sort of a little backup in case everything else went wrong.
And so then we were in Belgium.
And we were reunited with my father.
And we lived in a--
It must have been-- I-- I don't recall because he met us-- he met us when we got there.
As I understand from my cousin, it was this-- this business associate in-- in Spa that somehow worked it out that he was released from jail.

And in Spa, we-- we sort of languished for a bit [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] until perhaps early in June when we decided to move to Brussels because Spa was so close to the border that both my mother and father felt fairly uncomfortable-- in case something were to go wrong that we would be of-- further inland.
In Spa, it was more or less carefree.
We did not go to school in Spa.
And so we-- we played as children will and were in the park and lived in a furnished room and didn't have much.
There was not much going on at the time as far as we children were concerned.
I believe my parents continued to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to try and process papers and speed things up for our exit visas and things to the United States.
And that was the way we spent our time.
OK.
All right.
Thank you.
